{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[Neues Reich]] |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-M21-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''{{Unicode|Sb3-n-S3ḥ}}''
''Zehenstern / Fußstern'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild „Sah“ (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' ist der [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Name von [[Rigel]] (β Orionis), dem Hauptstern des altägyptischen [[Sternbild]]es [[Sah (Sternbild)|Sah]]. == Mythologische Verbindungen == Seba-en-Sah spielte als Namensgeber für das Sternbild Sah und als Dekanstern auf der Deckendarstellung im Grab des [[Senenmut]] sowie im [[Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)|Tagewählkalender]] eine wichtige Rolle. Am [[Achet#Datierungen|16. Achet III]] repräsentierte Seba-en-Sah die siebte Nachtstunde, fünfzehn Tage danach am 1. Achet IV die fünfte Nachtstunde.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 135. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der Dekan-Sterne im Nutbuch]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[Neues Reich]] |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-M21-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''{{Unicode|Sb3-n-S3ḥ}}''
''Zehenstern / Fußstern'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild „Sah“ (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (auch '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''Erster, die Zehe'') ist der [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Name von [[Rigel]] (β Orionis), dem Hauptstern des altägyptischen [[Sternbild]]es [[Sah (Sternbild)|Sah]]. == Mythologische Verbindungen == Seba-en-Sah spielte als Namensgeber für das Sternbild Sah und als Dekanstern auf der Deckendarstellung im Grab des [[Senenmut]] sowie im [[Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)|Tagewählkalender]] eine wichtige Rolle. Am [[Achet#Datierungen|16. Achet III]] repräsentierte Seba-en-Sah die siebte Nachtstunde, fünfzehn Tage danach am 1. Achet IV die fünfte Nachtstunde.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 135. [[Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)|Rolf Krauss]] lehnt die Gleichsetzung von Seba-en-Sah mit Rigel aufgrund seiner Ausführungen zum [[Sothis-Zyklus]] ab. Seine Annahme, dass für den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgang des Sirius zunächst [[Elephantine]] als [[Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge|Bezugsort]] gewählt wurde, schließt aus diesen Gründen die Zuordnung zu Rigel aus. „''Krauss muss dies tun,...da ansonsten seine gesamten Berechnungen hinfällig wären''“. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der Dekan-Sterne im Nutbuch]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[Neues Reich]] |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''{{Unicode|Sb3-n-S3ḥ}}''
''Stern des Herabkommenden (Zehen- oder Fußstern)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild „Sah“ (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (auch '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''Erster des Herabkommenden'') ist der [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Name von [[Rigel]] (β Orionis), dem Hauptstern des altägyptischen [[Sternbild]]es [[Sah (Sternbild)|Sah]]. == Mythologische Verbindungen == Seba-en-Sah spielte als Namensgeber für das Sternbild Sah und als Dekanstern auf der Deckendarstellung im Grab des [[Senenmut]] sowie im [[Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)|Tagewählkalender]] eine wichtige Rolle. Am [[Achet#Datierungen|16. Achet III]] repräsentierte Seba-en-Sah die siebte Nachtstunde, fünfzehn Tage danach am 1. Achet IV die fünfte Nachtstunde.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 135. [[Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)|Rolf Krauss]] lehnt die Gleichsetzung von Seba-en-Sah mit Rigel aufgrund seiner Ausführungen zum [[Sothis-Zyklus]] ab. Seine Annahme, dass für den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgang des Sirius zunächst [[Elephantine]] als [[Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge|Bezugsort]] gewählt wurde, schließt aus diesen Gründen die Zuordnung zu Rigel aus. „''Krauss muss dies tun,...da ansonsten seine gesamten Berechnungen hinfällig wären''“. [[Bild:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|Die astronomische Decke im Grab des Senenmut]] In den [[Ramessidische Sternuhren|ramessidischen Sternuhren]] ist für das Jahr 1462 v. Chr. die [[Mitternacht|mitternächtliche]] [[Kulmination (Astronomie)|Kulmination]] für den [[Achet#Datierungen|23. und 24. Achet III]] (14. und 15. November[[Julianischer Kalender|jul.]]) vermerkt. Bemerkenswert an diesem Datum ist die taggenaue Übereinstimmung mit den Angaben auf der Deckendarstellung im [[TT353|Grab des Senenmut]]. Es wird deshalb sicherlich kein Zufall sein, dass die astronomischen Deckendarstellungen im Grab des Senenemut als Namensgeber für Dekananordnungen fungierte, die auch in ramessidischer Zeit noch immer als Kopien weiterverwendet wurden. Ein weiterer Beweis für die Anfertigung von Kopien der Deckendarstellungen in späterer Zeit stellt das Fehlen des [[Planet]]en [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]] dar, obwohl der Mars in anderen Texten Berücksichtigung fand.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. ''{{Unicode|Sb3-n-S3ḥ}}''
''Stern des Herabkommenden (Zehen- oder Fußstern)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild „Sah“ (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (auch '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''Erster des Herabkommenden'') ist der [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Name von [[Rigel]] (β Orionis), dem Hauptstern des altägyptischen [[Sternbild]]es [[Sah (Sternbild)|Sah]]. == Mythologische Verbindungen == Seba-en-Sah spielte als Namensgeber für das Sternbild Sah und als Dekanstern auf der Deckendarstellung im Grab des [[Senenmut]] sowie im [[Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)|Tagewählkalender]] eine wichtige Rolle. Am [[Achet#Datierungen|16. Achet III]] repräsentierte Seba-en-Sah die siebte Nachtstunde, fünfzehn Tage danach am 1. Achet IV die fünfte Nachtstunde.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 135. [[Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)|Rolf Krauss]] lehnt die Gleichsetzung von Seba-en-Sah mit Rigel aufgrund seiner Ausführungen zum [[Sothis-Zyklus]] ab. Seine Annahme, dass für den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgang des Sirius zunächst [[Elephantine]] als [[Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge|Bezugsort]] gewählt wurde, schließt aus diesen Gründen die Zuordnung zu Rigel aus. „''Krauss muss dies tun,...da ansonsten seine gesamten Berechnungen hinfällig wären...Mittel dazu ist eine verzerrte und wesentliche Punkte unterschlagende Wiedergabe der anderen Argumentationen.'“. [[Bild:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|Die astronomische Decke im Grab des Senenmut]] In den [[Ramessidische Sternuhren|ramessidischen Sternuhren]] ist für das Jahr 1462 v. Chr. die [[Mitternacht|mitternächtliche]] [[Kulmination (Astronomie)|Kulmination]] für den [[Achet#Datierungen|23. und 24. Achet III]] (14. und 15. November[[Julianischer Kalender|jul.]]) vermerkt. Bemerkenswert an diesem Datum ist die taggenaue Übereinstimmung mit den Angaben auf der Deckendarstellung im [[TT353|Grab des Senenmut]]. Es wird deshalb sicherlich kein Zufall sein, dass die astronomischen Deckendarstellungen im Grab des Senenmut als Namensgeber für Dekananordnungen fungierten, die auch in ramessidischer Zeit noch immer als Kopien weiterverwendet wurden. Ein weiterer Beweis für die Anfertigung von Kopien der Deckendarstellungen in späterer Zeit stellt das Fehlen des [[Planet]]en [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]] dar, obwohl der Mars in anderen Texten Berücksichtigung fand.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Seine Annahme, dass für den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgang des Sirius zunächst [[Elephantine]] als [[Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge|Bezugsort]] gewählt wurde, schließt aus diesen Gründen die Zuordnung zu Rigel aus: {{Zitat|Krauss muss dies tun,...da ansonsten seine gesamten Berechnungen hinfällig wären...Mittel dazu ist eine verzerrte und wesentliche Punkte unterschlagende Wiedergabe der anderen Argumentationen.|Christian Leitz, Altägyptische Sternuhren}} [[Bild:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|Die astronomische Decke im Grab des Senenmut]] In den ramessidischen Sternuhren ist die [[Mitternacht|mitternächtliche]] [[Kulmination (Astronomie)|Kulmination]] für den [[Achet#Datierungen|23. und 24. Achet III]]14. und 15. November[[Julianischer Kalender|jul.]] im Jahr 1463 v. Chr. vermerkt. Bemerkenswert an diesem Datum ist die taggenaue Übereinstimmung mit den Angaben auf der Deckendarstellung im [[TT353|Grab des Senenmut]], dessen astronomische Anordnungen 1463 v. Chr. verfasst worden sein müssen. Es wird deshalb sicherlich kein Zufall sein, dass die astronomischen Deckendarstellungen im Grab des Senenmut als Namensgeber für Dekananordnungen fungierten, die auch in ramessidischer Zeit noch immer als Kopien weiterverwendet wurden. Ein weiterer Beweis für die Anfertigung von Kopien der Deckendarstellungen in späterer Zeit stellt das Fehlen des [[Planet]]en [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]] dar, obwohl der Mars in anderen Texten Berücksichtigung fand.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Seba-en-Sah
''{{Unicode|Sb3-n-S3ḥ}}''
''Stern des Herabkommenden (Zehen- oder Fußstern)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild „Sah“ (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (auch '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''Erster des Herabkommenden'') ist der [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Name von [[Rigel]] ([[Orion (Sternbild)#Himmelsobjekte|β Orionis]]), dem Hauptstern des altägyptischen [[Sternbild]]es [[Sah (Sternbild)|Sah]]. == Mythologische Verbindungen == Seba-en-Sah spielte als Namensgeber für das Sternbild Sah und als Dekanstern auf der Deckendarstellung im Grab des [[Senenmut]] sowie im [[Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)|Tagewählkalender]] eine wichtige Rolle. In den [[Ramessidische Sternuhren|ramessidischen Sternuhren]] repräsentierte Seba-en-Sah am [[Achet#Datierungen|1. Achet IV]] den Beginn der [[Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|fünften Nachtstunde]].Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 135. [[Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)|Rolf Krauss]] lehnt die Gleichsetzung von Seba-en-Sah mit Rigel aufgrund seiner Ausführungen zum [[Sothis-Zyklus]] ab. Seine Annahme, dass für den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgang des Sirius zunächst [[Elephantine]] als [[Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge|Bezugsort]] gewählt wurde, schließt aus diesen Gründen die Zuordnung zu Rigel aus: {{Zitat|Krauss muss dies tun,...da ansonsten seine gesamten Berechnungen hinfällig wären...Mittel dazu ist eine verzerrte und wesentliche Punkte unterschlagende Wiedergabe der anderen Argumentationen.|Christian Leitz, Altägyptische Sternuhren}} [[Bild:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|Die astronomische Decke im Grab des Senenmut]] In den ramessidischen Sternuhren ist die [[Mitternacht|mitternächtliche]] [[Kulmination (Astronomie)|Kulmination]] für den [[Achet#Datierungen|23. und 24. Achet III]]14. und 15. November[[Julianischer Kalender|jul.]] im Jahr 1463 v. Chr. vermerkt, die somit in der Mitte der [[Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|siebten Nachtstunde]] stattfand. Bemerkenswert an diesem Datum ist die taggenaue Übereinstimmung mit den Angaben auf der Deckendarstellung im [[TT353|Grab des Senenmut]], dessen astronomische Anordnungen 1463 v. Chr. verfasst worden sein müssen. Es wird deshalb sicherlich kein Zufall sein, dass die astronomischen Deckendarstellungen im Grab des Senenmut als Namensgeber für Dekananordnungen fungierten, die auch in ramessidischer Zeit noch immer als Kopien weiterverwendet wurden. Ein weiterer Beweis für die Anfertigung von Kopien der Deckendarstellungen in späterer Zeit stellt das Fehlen des [[Planet]]en [[Mars (Planet)|Mars]] dar, obwohl der Mars in anderen Texten Berücksichtigung fand.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 136. == Stundenansetzungen == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Beginn der altägyptischen Nachtstunden für Seba-en-Sah im Jahr 1463 v. Chr.''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Nachtstunde !style="background:#FFE39B"|[[Ägyptischer Kalender]] !style="background:#FFE39B"|[[Gregorianischer Kalender]] |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|12]] |[[Achet#Datierungen|1. Achet I]] |11. August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|10]] |1. Achet II |10. September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|8]] |1. Achet III |10. Oktober |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7]] |16. Achet III |25. Oktober |- |} == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Dekanstern (Altägypten)]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[Neues Reich]] |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[New Kingdom of Egypt|Neues Reich]] |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; "First of the Descending") is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Sah (Sternbild)|de|Sah}}. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the constellation Sah and played a prominent role as a decan star depicted on the ceiling in the {{ill|TT353|de|Grab des Senenmut}} and in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Tagewählkalender}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside Star Clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah marked the start of the {{ill|Fifth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on the {{ill|Achet#Dating|1. Achet IV|de|1. Achet IV}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss (Egyptologist)|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} contested the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel due to his analysis of the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He proposed that {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}} served as the {{ill|Calendarical reference for Sothis risings|de|Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge}}, leading to discrepancies in the association with Rigel: {{Zitat |Text=Krauss must make this assertion,...otherwise his entire calculations would collapse... The method involves a distorted and significantly incomplete presentation of alternative arguments. |Autor=Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|Astronomical ceiling in the Tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside Star Clocks, the {{ill|Culmination (Astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} at midnight is recorded for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|23. and 24. Achet III|de|23. und 24. Achet III}},14. and 15. November (Julian calendar) in 1463 BCE. aligning with the {{ill|Seventh Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. This precise dating corresponds with the ceiling representation in the {{ill|TT353|de|Grab des Senenmut}}, which must have been created in 1463 BCE. The continued use of these astronomical depictions as templates in Ramesside times demonstrates the enduring influence of these layouts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. Notably, the absence of {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)}} in these depictions, despite its inclusion in other texts, supports the idea of later copying. == Night Hour Timings == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Gregorian Calendar |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Twelfth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|12]] |[[Achet#Dating|1. Achet I]] |11. August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Tenth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|10]] |1. Achet II |10. September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Eighth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|8]] |1. Achet III |10. October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Seventh Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|7]] |16. Achet III |25. October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[New Kingdom of Egypt|Neues Reich]] |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; "First of the Descending") is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Sah (Sternbild)|de|Sah}}. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the constellation Sah and played a prominent role as a decan star depicted on the ceiling in the {{ill|TT353|de|Grab des Senenmut}} and in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Tagewählkalender}}. Achet IV}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss (Egyptologist)|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} contested the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel due to his analysis of the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He proposed that {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}} served as the {{ill|Calendarical reference for Sothis risings|de|Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge}}, leading to discrepancies in the association with Rigel: {{Zitat |Text=Krauss must make this assertion,...otherwise his entire calculations would collapse... The method involves a distorted and significantly incomplete presentation of alternative arguments. |Autor=Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|Astronomical ceiling in the Tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside Star Clocks, the {{ill|Culmination (Astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} at midnight is recorded for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|23. and 24. Achet III|de|23. und 24. Achet III}},14. and 15. November (Julian calendar) in 1463 BCE. aligning with the {{ill|Seventh Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. This precise dating corresponds with the ceiling representation in the {{ill|TT353|de|Grab des Senenmut}}, which must have been created in 1463 BCE. The continued use of these astronomical depictions as templates in Ramesside times demonstrates the enduring influence of these layouts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. Notably, the absence of {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)}} in these depictions, despite its inclusion in other texts, supports the idea of later copying. == Night Hour Timings == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Gregorian Calendar |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Twelfth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|12]] |[[Achet#Dating|1. Achet I]] |11. August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Tenth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|10]] |1. Achet II |10. September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Eighth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|8]] |1. Achet III |10. October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Seventh Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|7]] |16. Achet III |25. October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[New Kingdom of Egypt|Neues Reich]] |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; "First of the Descending") is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the ancient Egyptian {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild}} {{ill|Sah (Constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)|lt=Sah}}. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the constellation Sah and played a prominent role as a decan star depicted on the ceiling in the {{ill|TT353|de|Grab des Senenmut}} and in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Tagewählkalender}}. Achet IV}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss (Egyptologist)|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} contested the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel due to his analysis of the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He proposed that {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}} served as the {{ill|Calendarical reference for Sothis risings|de|Bezugsort der kalendarischen Sothis-Aufgänge}}, leading to discrepancies in the association with Rigel: {{Zitat |Text=Krauss must make this assertion,...otherwise his entire calculations would collapse... The method involves a distorted and significantly incomplete presentation of alternative arguments. |Autor=Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|Astronomical ceiling in the Tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside Star Clocks, the {{ill|Culmination (Astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} at midnight is recorded for the {{ill|Achet#Dating|23. and 24. Achet III|de|23. und 24. Achet III}},14. and 15. November (Julian calendar) in 1463 BCE. aligning with the {{ill|Seventh Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. This precise dating corresponds with the ceiling representation in the {{ill|TT353|de|Grab des Senenmut}}, which must have been created in 1463 BCE. The continued use of these astronomical depictions as templates in Ramesside times demonstrates the enduring influence of these layouts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. Notably, the absence of {{ill|Mars (planet)|de|Mars (Planet)}} in these depictions, despite its inclusion in other texts, supports the idea of later copying. == Night Hour Timings == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Gregorian Calendar |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Twelfth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|12]] |[[Achet#Dating|1. Achet I]] |11. August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Tenth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|10]] |1. Achet II |10. September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |[[Eighth Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|8]] |1. Achet III |10. October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |[[Seventh Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|7]] |16. Achet III |25. October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}} |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. ## Mythological Connections Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the {{ill|planet}} {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. ## Hourly Assignments {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} ## Literature * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 ## References [[Category:Dekanstern (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Seba-en-Sah]] [[de:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]]

In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination (astronomy)|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the {{ill|planet}} {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}} |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 === References === [[Category:Dekanstern (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Seba-en-Sah]] [[de:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]]

''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. ''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 === References === [[Category:Dekanstern (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Seba-en-Sah]] [[de:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:properly attributed pages]]

{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}} |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 === References === [[Category:Dekanstern (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[en:draft:Seba-en-Sah]] [[de:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:properly attributed pages]]

Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}} |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 === References === [[Category:Dekanstern (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Decan star (Ancient Egypt)]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[en:draft:Seba-en-Sah]] [[de:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Seba-en-Sah]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Seba-en-Sah]]

''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}} |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}} |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}} |NAME = S29-Aa17-V28-D61-N14:N35
Seba-en-Sah
''Sb3-n-S3ḥ''
''Star of the Descending One (Toe or Foot Star)'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 250px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Constellation "Sah" (Orion) }} '''Seba-en-Sah''' (also '''Tepi-a Sah'''; ''First of the Descending One'') is the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten}} name for {{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel (Stern)}} ({{ill|Beta Orionis|de|β Orionis}}), the principal star of the {{ill|Sah (constellation)|de|Sah (Sternbild)}} constellation in ancient Egyptian astronomy. == Mythological Connections == Seba-en-Sah served as the namesake for the Sah constellation and as a dekan star depicted on the astronomical ceiling of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}’s tomb and in the {{ill|day selection calendar|de|Tagewählkalender (Altes Ägypten)}}. In the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}, Seba-en-Sah represented the beginning of the {{ill|Fifth Hour of the Night|de|Fünfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}} on {{ill|1 Achet IV|de|Achet#Datierungen|1=Season_of_the_Inundation#Months|lt=Achet}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 135. {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss (Ägyptologe)}} rejected the identification of Seba-en-Sah with Rigel based on his findings about the {{ill|Sothic cycle|de|Sothis-Zyklus}}. He argued that the initial reference point for the heliacal rise of Sirius was {{ill|Elephantine|de|Elephantine}}, excluding the possibility of identifying Seba-en-Sah with Rigel: {{Zitat|Krauss must assert this,… otherwise his entire calculations would collapse… The means involve a distorted and substantially incomplete representation of alternative arguments.|Christian Leitz, ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''}} [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb of Senenmut]] In the Ramesside star clocks, the {{ill|midnight|de|Mitternacht}} {{ill|culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} of Seba-en-Sah is recorded for {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen|1=Season_of_the_Inundation#Months|lt=Achet}}.14 and 15 November {{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}} in 1463 BCE. This culmination occurred during the middle of the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}. Remarkably, this date aligns precisely with the details found on the astronomical ceiling of the {{ill|Tomb TT353|de|TT353}} (Senenmut’s tomb), suggesting the astronomical arrangements were drawn up in 1463 BCE. It is therefore no coincidence that the astronomical ceiling in Senenmut’s tomb served as a template for dekan arrangements, which continued to be copied during the Ramesside period. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen|1=Season_of_the_Inundation#Months|lt=Achet}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Another piece of evidence supporting the copying of such ceilings in later periods is the absence of the [[:en:Planet|planet]] {{ill|Mars|de|Mars (Planet)}}, even though Mars appears in other texts.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 136. == Hourly Assignments == {| class="wikitable center" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} |colspan="3" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Start of Ancient Egyptian Night Hours for Seba-en-Sah in 1463 BCE''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|Night Hour !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} !style="background:#FFE39B"|{{ill|Gregorian calendar|de|Gregorianischer Kalender}} |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Twelfth hour of the night (Ancient Egypt)|lt=Twelfth Hour of the Night|de|Zwölfte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=12}} |{{ill|1 Achet I|de|Achet#Datierungen|1=Season_of_the_Inundation#Months|lt=Achet|lt=1 Achet I}} |11 August |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Tenth Hour of the Night|de|Zehnte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=10}} |1 Achet II |10 September |- bgcolor="#fffaf0" |{{ill|Eighth Hour of the Night|de|Achte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=8}} |1 Achet III |10 October |- bgcolor="#f5f5f5" |{{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|lt=7}} |16 Achet III |25 October |- |} == Literature == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''.
